text.title: Repository, Specifications, and Transactional Service
text.summary: JpaRepository with JpaSpecificationExecutor, Specification factory methods, and service impl with proper @Transactional defaults.

rule.statement: Extend JpaSpecificationExecutor for dynamic queries. Use Specification factory methods in a separate specification class. Never build JPQL strings by concatenation.
rule.rationale: String concatenation in JPQL opens SQL injection vectors and is untyped. Specifications are type-safe, composable, and testable independently.

rule.statement: Annotate service class with @Transactional(readOnly = true). Override with @Transactional (no readOnly) on individual write methods. Never apply @Transactional to private methods or call them directly on the same bean.
rule.rationale: @Transactional on private methods is silently ignored — Spring AOP proxies intercept only public method calls from outside the bean. Self-invocation (this.writeMethod()) bypasses the proxy and runs without a transaction.

antipattern.description: Iterating over a collection and accessing a lazy-loaded relationship: users.forEach(u -> u.getOrganization().getName()).
antipattern.reason: Each getOrganization() triggers a separate SELECT. For N users, N+1 queries are issued. Use JOIN FETCH or @EntityGraph to load relationships in one query.

example.code: @Query("SELECT u FROM User u JOIN FETCH u.organization WHERE u.id IN :ids") List<User> findAllWithOrganization(@Param("ids") List<UUID> ids);
